text: Common Hand Conditions and Their Treatment Information to Accompany Hand Lecture for University of Western Australia Students Paul Jarrett FRACS Text and figure 1 Copyright Paul Jarrett Hand and Upper Limb Surgeon August 2008 Murdoch Orthopaedic Clinic and Fremantle Hospital www.pauljarrett.info Overview The hand is an important part of the body and its dysfunction can be very symptomatic. You not only use your hands for functional activities but also your hands, along with your face, are the other area of your body normally on display and are used for communication and for social activities; hand deformities and to a lesser extent dysfunction are often glaringly obvious to all around the patient. In the lecture we are going to discuss the anatomy including surface anatomy, injuries to the hand and the significance of the surface anatomy along with the first aid of hand injuries, the importance of prevention of swelling and correct splintage of the hand and then finally we will cover the more common non- emergency condition relating to the hand. Anatomy In an hour lecture we can only delve superficially into hand anatomy. There are many structures in the hand tightly packed together and their coordinated function is vital to proper hand function. Skin, tendons, nerves, blood vessels, ligaments, cartilage and bone can all become pathologic and prevent effective use of the hand. The skin surface markings are important as dysfunction of the hand often has an anatomical basis. In the hand it is most common to be able to identify the pathological area from clinical examination and therefore if you understand the deep anatomy you are one step closer to understanding which structures may be responsible. One example of how surface anatomy differs from what is intuitive is that the metacarpophalyngeal joints are easy to identify dorsally, but volvarly where do they lie? Look at your own hands; they are actually a good way down the palm with the little and ring finger metacarpophalyngeal joint (MCPJs) underlying the distal palmar crease, the middle finger MCPJ being between the distal and proximal palmar creases and the index finger MCPJ being at the level of the proximal palmar crease. The surface anatomy at the carpus is arguably even more important to know and it is worth having a think about where each bone and each joint lies as all the areas can be easily and individually palpated to assess for tenderness suggestive of pathology. Figure 1. Surface anatomy og the volar aspect of the hand. Note the surface anatomy of the MCP joints compared to the skin creases. The functional anatomy is also important and the following covers a few well known and important features: • The median nerve innervates the radial three and a half or occasionally radial two and a half digits volarly with the ulnar nerve innervating the corresponding ulnar fingers. The superficial radial nerve covers the radial side of the dorsum of the hand and digits but there is often a considerable overlap between nerves; the base of the thumb dorsally is usually however only innervated by the superficial radial nerve. • There are two flexor tendons to each digit (although occasionally only the profundus to the little finger with an absent superficialis). The flexor digitorum profundus (FDP) flexes the whole digit including the distal interphalyngeal joint (DIPJ) and the flexor digitorum superficialis (FDS) tendon can flex the proximal interphalyngeal joint (PIPJ) but not the DIPJ. So how can you test if a FDS tendon is ruptured when an FDP tendon is still intact? The answer to this is that the muscle bellies of the FDP are combined in the forearm (although often excluding that to the index finger) and by keeping the digits either side passively fully extended you can assess isolated FDS function of each finger. • The extensor tendon apparatus in the finger is a complex structure but the more important areas are the central slip (coming from the main extensor tendon from the forearm) which goes to the base of the middle phalynx to extend the PIPJ and then the two lateral bands are attached into the base of the distal phalynx to allow extension of the DIPJ. It is possible to injury both or either of these structures or some of the individual smaller aspects of the extensor tendon to give particular patterns of dysfunction or deformity. We will cover some of these later. History Naturally, like nearly all areas of medicine, a history is important in hand diagnosis. • What are the patient’s complaints? • Are the complaints getting better, worse, static or changing in nature? • Injury – has an injury occurred, when, where, what mechanism, how much force, and all factors relating to the injury. • Pain – where, when, what type? • Numbness – where, when, how significant? • Dysfunction – this is important; how is the hand problem affecting the patient’s life including ADLs, work, leisure and family pursuits? • Systemic problems – significant medical problems are relevant including diabetes. • Smoking – does the patient smoke? There are some problems made worse by smoking and some procedures with a significant failure rate is smokes. For instance we would be much less likely to perform free tissue transfer into the hand in a smoker. • Patient factors – age, occupation, handedness, ethnic factors if appropriate • Treatment – what treatment has the patient already had and how effective was it? • When did the patient last eat / drink (after acute injury)? Examination Look – hand position; is the position of the resting wrist and fingers natural and comparable to the other side or to that of a normal hand and wrist? Are there any deformities? Are there any scars, callosities, abnormal colour / texture / skin growth? Is the sweating pattern normal throughout the hands? Feel – palpate all areas of the finger(s) / hand / wrists that are clinically important based on the history. You can usually be very specific regarding the areas of palpation in relation to the underlying anatomical structures. Move – request the patient to clench their fist, open their fingers out, and spread theirs fingers out. This is a good screening test to exclude significant motion loss. Then more careful assessment of each digit and the wrist and rest of the upper limb can be undertaken as required based on the history. Passive motion if not too painful can be assessed when active motion is not present. Special tests – these will be guided by the clinical history and proceeding examination and will target the possibly pathologies present. For instance, Tinel’s and Phalen’s test will be requires for suspected carpal tunnel syndrome, Allen’s test of the wrist or digits will be required for suspected vascular insufficiency, and carpal instability tests required if this pathology is suspected. Initial Management of Injury In acute injuries, in addition to a history and examination, basic first aid will be required. If more significant injuries are present, especially potentially life or health threatening ones then naturally these must be managed based on their priority for the patient. The hand should be dressed if there are open wounds, ideally with a damp non- adhesive dressing with sufficient compression to prevent undue blood loss but not to prevent blood flow getting to the hand or causing distal swelling. Tetanus prophylaxis and antibiotics will be required and the hand elevated unless there is a significant vascular injury causing critical ischaemia of the injured part in which case a hand position at the level of the heart will be better. Splinting will be required to reduce pain and swelling if there are fractures or significant soft tissue injury and analgesics will be required. Investigations Most hands that have sustained a blunt injury, in those where fractures or dislocations are suspected and when contamination of wounds with foreign material is possible will require plain x-rays. Many elective conditions also require x-rays including the investigation of arthritis. CTs are useful for defining the anatomy of known fractures and at times useful for assessment of occult fractures; note that occult scaphoid fractures are best assessed with isotope bone scanning or MRI. Ultrasound can detect foreign bodies, tendon and ligament injuries but other than for these reasons its use is less common in the hand compared to many other areas of the body. MRI is useful for the assessment of occult bony injuries, injuries to tendons, ligaments and for assessing vascularisation of the proximal pole in scaphoid fractures. However, most often the above injuries can be detected by clinical examination and a set of plain x-rays and especially in the acute setting there is relatively little requirement for MRI. Nuclear medicine imaging can be useful to detect occult bony injuries and infections and at time for locating the source of a chronic painful condition. Splinting of the hand The hand after injury often becomes swollen. The resulting tissue fluid contains a significant level of fibrin and causes adhesion / scarring within the hand. Normal movement of the fingers and hand require the gliding of structures one over another and this adhesion or scarring will prevent motion from occurring. Much of a hand practitioners work attempts to reverse the swelling and the effects of the swelling. Elevation and splinting of the hand early in the management of a hand injury can make a dramatic short and long term difference to the end results for the patient and considerably shorten the recovery period. There are a number of positions in which the hand can be splinted. The proximal and distal interphalyngeal joints are different in their designs from the metacarpophalyngeal joints due to the shape of the joint. Joints should be splinted in the position in which their collateral ligaments are longest, to prevent stiffness. For the MCPJs this is in flexion and for the DIP / PIP joints this is in extension. Therefore in a swollen hand it is generally better to splint the hand in this way, often called the intrinsic plus position or safe position of the hand. Safe position / “intrinsic plus” of the hand with at least 70 degrees flexion at the MCP and extended IP joints The other important consideration in splinting is to protect a specific injured structure and this will make priority of the safe position until definitive management is undertaken. For instance, in a finger fracture a splint should be applied to keep the finger in a comfortable and non-deformed position and this takes priority over the safe position if this cannot also be achieved in the splint. Flexor Tendon Injuries Flexor tendons are most often injured by sharp laceration. It is also possible for them to rupture in a closed manner usually being avulsed from their distal attachment especially for the FDP tendons. Finally in inflammatory arthropathies and very occasionally osteoarthritis of the wrist and occasionally after fractures of the wrist, tendons can rupture due to synovitis or wear over a sharp edge of bone. Tendon lacerations or ruptures will require repair or reconstruction and then careful splinting for six weeks with hand therapy to help regain motion without adhesion formation occurring and without rupturing of the repaired tendon. There is an increased risk of adhesion formation occurring in the area of the flexor fibrous sheath / pulley system in the fingers where both FDS and FDP are present. Diagram of Modified Kessler tendon repair – a common flexor tendon repair technique. Extensor Tendon Injuries It is often thought that pathology in the extensor tendon system is less important than that in the flexor system as many students / doctors believe the flexor system is more complex and that flexion is a more important function than extension. However the extensor mechanism is actually more complex than the flexor system and a good quality repair actually more difficult to achieve. Secondly, whilst it is uncommon following extensor tendon pathology and repair not to regain a good level of extension, adhesions in the extensor system commonly prevent flexion of the digit occurring. Therefore extensor tendon problems should be considered a serious problem and treated in at least as competently a manner as for flexor pathology. Extensor tendon rupture is not uncommon in inflammatory arthropathies but often missed if not thought of and looked for in these patients. Mallet Finger A mallet finger is the name given to an injury to the terminal insertion of the extensor mechanism of the finger. This is most commonly an avulsion of the tendon from the bone but can alternatively involve an area of bone being avulsed from the dorsal aspect of the distal phalynx. These injuries are almost exclusively treated in a splint after x-rays to ensure that there is not a fracture is greater than 50% of the joint surface or no joint subluxation (both possible reasons to consider surgery). The mechanism by which the splint works is by opposing the tendon edges / bone edges and allowing healing. To achieve this, the DIP joint has to be fully extended 24/7 for at six to eight weeks. The splint can be removed to wash the finger keeping the digit passively fully extended throughout the wash, as you will be shown how to do this during the lecture. Mallet finger tendon appearance and splint Boutonniere Deformity When the central slip of the extensor tendon is ruptured (trauma or laceration) or becomes deficient (e.g rheumatoid disease) then a typical deformity develops sometimes rapidly but often over some weeks or months. The PIPJ flexes and cannot fully actively straighten and the DIPJ becomes hyper-extended. The DIP hyperextension is due to the proximal migration of the extensor apparatus. After acute injury this pathology can usually be treated with a splint for six weeks designed by the hand therapists. If the condition is chronic, surgery will most likely be required. Boutonniere Deformity Neurovascular bundle injury Often when patients lacerate a finger they will tell you about the rapid nature of the bleeding and in this case most likely one of the volar arteries was lacerated. Unlike in the palm where the artery is superficial to the nerve, in the digit the nerve lies superficial to the nerve and therefore if there has been arterial bleeding most often the associated digital nerve has also been lacerated. Nerves when lacerated and subsequently repaired will never return to their pre- injury function long term and no recovery will be seen for a small number of months. From a hand surgeon’s viewpoint it is important to let patient know of this prior to surgical repair as patients would otherwise be disappointed by their early apparent poor results. Hand skin defect management Skin is the largest organ in the body and is as essential for effective function of the hand. When a hand injury or infection has caused a loss of skin there are a number of methods by which the area of skin loss can be covered. These start with very simple solutions and can work up to complex solutions as required. Clearly, the simplest method applicable to achieving one’s aims is ideal and therefore the list of possible options is called “the reconstruction ladder” and if the first rung of the ladder will not solve your problem then the doctor continues going up the rungs of the ladder until they can achieve a solution to the skin loss. The options on the ladder include: leaving alone to granulate via dressings, dressings and VAC pump, skin grafting, dermal substitutes and grafting, local pedicle flaps and free tissue transfer. The lecture will include pictures of these various options. Nailbed Injuries The finger tip is one of the most commonly injured areas of the body and the nailbed is a common area affected by this trauma. The nail may be avulsed or there may be a subungual haematoma. Should nailbed lacerations fail to heal normally then secondary disturbance to nail growth can occur. Therefore any nailbed laceration other than trivial ones should be considered for surgical repair under magnification. Fractures Hand fractures is a large topic and it is not possible to cover this area during the hour lecture extensively. Therefore this handout will cover hand fractures in slightly more detail. Carpal Fracture Fractures can occur of all the carpal bones but the scaphoid is by far more common. Please see appendix 1 for a relatively comprehensive guide to management of potential scaphoids in the primary care setting. Hook of hamate fractures are common following a blow or repetitive blows to the hypothenar eminence and are common in Golfers. The Hook is palpable in the palm approximately 15 mm along a line from the pisiform towards the base of the index finger. These fractures have a high non-union rate and if they fail to heal in plaster are usually excised. Avulsion fractures from the dorsum of the triquetrum are common and are usually treated by immobilisation for 4-6 weeks. Other fractures are managed according to the degree of displacement and or comminution and often require CT to fully document the anatomy of the fracture or to identify the fracture. Metacarpal Fractures Base fractures of the metacarpal bones if intra-articular and undisplaced can be splinted and watched with serial x-rays to ensure the fractures do not displace. Displaced fractures clearly require operative reduction and fixation. Fractures of the shafts of the thumb / index and little fingers if displaced or angulated require reduction and fixation and otherwise if there is no displacement require splintage for about 4 weeks. The middle and ring finger metacarpals are splinted by the surrounding metacarpals and are much less likely to require operation. One common fracture is a flexed fracture of the neck of the little finger metacarpus usually cause by striking an object (e.g wall, door, someone’s head by punching). This is the so-called “Boxer’s fracture” and these fractures are usually managed non-operatively with neighbor strapping or thermoplastic splint if the angle is under 45 degrees and some surgeons feel infact 70 degrees. Phalyngeal Fractures If there is any rotational deformity, if there is displacement of an intra-articular fracture or more than mild angulation or translation at a fracture it will require reduction with possible fixation. Otherwise these fractures can be treated wiith conservative methods which may include neighbor strapping or splinting with thermoplastic splints. One question often asked is how do you determine if a digit is mal-rotated? There are two good methods of this. One is to see where the fingers point to on the palm when flexed; all the digits should point to the scaphoid tubercle as in the diagram below. However, if it is not possible to flex the affected digit(s) due to pain and stiffness, another method is to look at the finger nails end on of both hands which form a gentle arc in their change of rotation and if there is no gentle arc or difference between sides this indicates likely mal-rotation. All fingers when flexed should point to scaphoid tubercle Amputations Amputated fingers / hand / forearm / arm may be suitable for reimplantation. Should a patient suffer an amputation a sterile dressing should be placed on the stump of the patient and them given tetanus cover and antibiotics. The nearest hand referral centre should be contacted very urgently and advice sought and usually emergency transfer of the patient and the injured part arranged. The injured part must be kept cold but not frozen. To achieve this the part is wrapped in cool moist swabs containing sterile solution (anything suitable for intravenous use is acceptable especially normal saline), placed in a container such as a clear plastic bag and then placed in a further bag or box of crushed ice. Remember the part must accompany the patient (I have had many patients without parts coming with them which results in a less than good outcome). The decision whether to re-implant the injured part will be determined by many factors. Heavily damaged or contamined parts and parts with a long ischaemic time are less likely to be suitable. Patients who have other more important life threatening injuries are not suitable for implantation. Also, rehabilitation from these operations is long and the results not normal and at times it is better for the patient not to have a re-implant; we do not put things back on just because we can. It has to be better for the patient. However, we will nearly always try to put thumbs, multiple digits and children’s parts back on as patients do much better following these operations than from not undertaking them in these circumstances. Ligamentous Injuries in the Wrist There are a large collection of ligaments around and in the wrist which along with the bones, muscle-tendon units and coordination due to proprioceptive-peripheral / central nervous system involvement allow normal function of the wrist. These ligaments can be injured and cause significant symptoms. This topic is very large and progressing rapidly and therefore I will only mention the most common and significant injury which is scapholunate ligament injury. The scapholunate ligament is injured in a similar manner to the scaphoid usually with a fall onto an outstretched hand with resultant wrist discomfort and stiffness. X-rays will not show fracture unless there are co-existent fractures which can occur especially of the distal radius and scaphoid. There is normally some degree of dorsal wrist swelling and tenderness dorsally over the ligament. At time on x-rays there may be a larger gap visible than normal between the scaphoid and lunate (the upper limit of normal is 3mm). When clinically suspected this injury should be followed up with either review of a hand surgeon or imaging in the form of ultrasound by an experienced musculoskeletal radiologist or MRI. When present these ligaments are treated with surgical repair acutely or reconstruction chronically. These injuries do MUCH better being treated early and this is where it helps to have high index of suspicion regarding them. Ulnar collateral ligament injury of the thumb MCP joint This injury is caused by radial deviation of the thumb and is an incapacitating injury. In the acute form it can be called a “Skiier’s thumb” and in the chronic form it can be called a “Gamekeeper’s thumb”. Usually the ligament is avulsed from the bone but a small or medium sized fragment of bone may be avulsed with the ligament and therefore visible on x-rays and for this reason and to exclude a more significant fracture x-rays should be undertaken. There is a difference in stressing the ligament between sides on clinically examination and if there is over 15 degrees difference between sides this indicates a significant injury. When the injuries are partial sprains with no or under 15 degrees laxity then the injury can be treated by splinting for six weeks in a thermoplastic splint or cast and if the injury is a full ligament tear then the most reliable way of achieving a good result is surgical repair. One reason that splint treatment of a full ligament sprain may fail is because of the “Stener lesion”. When the ligament is avulsed it is usually avulsed from the proximal phalynx and if pulled proximally enough the adductor aponeurosis of the thumb may lie between the ruptured ends of the ligament and therefore prevent healing of the ligament. Carpal Tunnel Syndrome This condition represents compression of the median nerve in the wrist in the carpal tunnel. The symptoms consist of pins and needles in the median innervated area of the hand with discomfort in the wrist often radiating into the forearm and occasionally more proximally and often a feeling of hand weakness or clumsiness. Signs may include a positive Tinel’s signs or Phalen’s test at the wrist and wasting of the abductor pollicis brevis muscle in more severe cases. In cases where the diagnosis is in doubt nerve conduction studies can be organised but if the diagnosis is clear cut from history and examination then many practitioners feel nerve conduction studies do not add to the diagnosis. Treatment consists of night splints and nerve gliding exercises initially with activity modification and sometimes NSAIDs but if this fails to improve symptoms then a carpal tunnel decompression is the best option to relieve the symptoms. Carpal tunnel decompression can often be undertaken under local anaesthetic and is a relatively low risk operation with a rapid recovery in the majority of patients. Cubital Tunnel Syndrome (Ulnar nerve compression at the elbow) The ulnar nerve can become compressed or ischaemic at the elbow which causes symptoms including discomfort at the medial side of the elbow and pins and needles of the ulnar innervated skin in the hand with at time weakness of the hand. Their symptoms like carpal tunnel syndrome are often worse at night and also during activities which cause prolonged flexion of the elbow such as reading, driving or speaking on the telephone. Patients may be tender over their ulnar nerve at the elbow with a positive Tinel’s. There may be evidence of ulnar nerve dysfunction in the hand with wasting of the ulnar innervated intrinsic muscles in more severe cases. Nerve conduction studies are usually requested to confirm the diagnosis although these studies can often be normal even in a clear cut case. Treatment usually starts with night elbow extension splinting and nerve gliding exercises under the supervision of the hand therapists. Should this fail then surgical decompression can be considered with or without anterior transposition of the ulnar nerve (the latter allows the nerve to take a shorter course through the elbow, prevents nerve subluxation and can further decompress the nerve). Trigger Digits Triggering digits are another hand pathology the basis of which has not been understood properly but thankfully the treatment is very effective. The flexor tendons run in a fibrous flexor sheath in the fingers as you know. The mouth of the A1 pulley tends to be slightly narrower than the rest of the pulley. In a trigger digit, a swelling develops in the flexor tendons which catch at the A1 pulley causing a clicking sensation. Often patients will flex their finger which then gets stuck in flexion and the patient has to manually straighten their finger past the point which it catches in order to extend the digit fully again. A nodule is often palpable in the palm which moves with the tendon and can be mildly tender in acute cases. The condition is more common in diabetics and patients with multiple trigger fingers may benefit from checking of their glucose levels to exclude diabetes. Treatment can consist of a hand therapy splint in acute cases, or a steroid injection or surgical release of the A1 pulley. Steroid injection is normally the first line of treatment as 75% of patients will resolve with one corticosteroid injection although with time a small proportion of those with recovery will re-develop the condition. Ganglions Ganglions are small synovial cysts which can occur in many areas of the body and are common in the hand and wrist. The true nature and cause for their development has never been fully explained and their optimal treatment is controversial. Ganglions in the wrist and hand are most common on the dorsal aspect of the wrist, on the volar and radial aspect of the wrist, on the volar aspect of the base of the fingers near the MCPJ crease and dorsally around the DIPJ of the hand associated with degenerative joint disease of the DIPJ. They appear as soft non- tender non-fixed transilluminating lesions and if required can be assessed with ultrasound. Often, especially with time they disappear. Patients can get an aching associated with them and occasionally symptoms may be overtly painful. Patients often do not like the appearance of the lesion and at times patients will claim their symptoms to be more than they are in reality to justify surgical treatment of the ganglion. Treatment options include leaving the ganglion alone, aspirating it, aspirating it and injecting corticosteroid, excising them open or arthroscopically. Surgery carries a high recurrence rate especially if follow-up is over a large number of years. My personal suggestion is to leave them alone initially unless very symptomatic or large. If very symptomatic or large I would remove them arthroscopically if possible as the recurrence rates may be lower and the scars smaller. If the ganglions are quite symptomatic, it is prudent to investigate the patient further to exclude additional pathology that might be causing the symptoms. A plain x-ray would be a minimum and added to this might be an ultrasound / MRI or arthroscopy. It is possible to have occult ganglion in the wrist hidden beneath the retinaculum which are symptomatic. Likewise, there are also cases in which investigation for wrist pain reveals an occult ganglion which is coincidental and not causing the patient’s symptoms. Dupuytren’s Disease / Contracture Dupuytren’s disease is principally a disease of the fibrous tissue in the palm of the hand forming small nodules in the palm of the hand with or without small skin puts and long cord structures which can cause fixed flexion contractures of the fingers. There is a strong genetic component to this condition originally from northern European especially of Celtic or Viking origin. The pathophysiology is that of fibroblast becoming myofibroblasts and thickening and causing contracture of the affected tissue. There is currently no preventative treatment but treatment options do exist for pre-existing or developing disease. At times when a nodule is developing it can become quite painful and tender and these become painfree with time. However during the painful phase a steroid injection into the nodule can reduce the tenderness and swelling associated with the inflammation. Once a contracture has developed so that the patient cannot get their hand flat on the table (table top test) then surgery can be considered. Also any significant PIPJ contracture deserved consideration for surgery. Surgery can take the form of percutaneous needle fasciotomy (performed by a surgeon), fasciectomy (an open removal of the affected fascia) or a dermofasciectomy (fasciotomy with removal of overlying skin and skin grafting of the affected area). Recurrence is common after treatment with 50% of patients treated with fasciotomy recurring within 3 years to some extent and 50% of patients treated with fasciectomy recurring within 5 years to some extent. Garrod’s pads are a firm thickening in the dorsal skin overlying the PIPJs in some patients with Dupuytren’s disease especially for those more severely affected. It generally requires no treatment. Leiderhosen’s disease is Dupuytren’s disease of the plantar fascia of the foot. This uncommonly requires surgical resection and is much less common than Dupuytren’s of the hand Peroniere’s disease is Dupuytren’s contracture of the penis. It is again less common than that in the hand or foot and it can cause erection to be uncomfortable and for the penis to deviate to one side. This condition is managed when required by urologists and in patients with very marked Dupuytren’s disease of the hand as they may have penile involvement which is causing them problems but they are too embarrassed to mention it to their doctor. APPENDIX 1 THE LOW STRESS GUIDE TO SCAPHOID FRACTURE MANAGEMENT Whilst some fractures are relatively benign, scaphoid fractures are usually more troublesome: They are often not visible on x-rays, take a considerable time to unite and have a high rate of non-union. However, tackled in a systematic manner using an evidence based approach and modern imaging techniques many of the problems and pitfalls of scaphoid injuries can be annulled or reduced. PRESENTATION - Scaphoid fractures are usually the result of a fall onto an outstretched hand and patients present with a painful stiff wrist with tenderness over the affected part of the scaphoid and pain on circumduction and axial thumb compression. A different clinical presentation makes a scaphoid fracture less likely, but still possible. One dilemma is that the above presentation is common but the majority of such patients do not have scaphoid fractures – so how do you ensure you are treating the fractures and not over-treating the mild wrist sprain? CONFIRMING FRACTURE - If a patient had any possibility of a scaphoid fracture you would naturally refer them for a x-ray. However, x-rays only demonstrate a fracture in under 90% of patients who have a scaphoid fracture. If you clinically suspect there could be a scaphoid fracture but none is present on x-ray then there are two approaches: A) Traditional method - splint the wrist and undertake further x-rays at an interval of 10 to 14 days with a repeat clinical examination. If there are no clinical signs of a fracture and the radiographs are normal it is reasonable to treat the patient symptomatically. If a fracture is evident on x-rays it can be treated and if clinically a fracture is still potentially present and x-rays are normal, further I imaging is required. B) Modern method - a second option which I would recommend as being superior, is to continue to splint the patient’s wrist and to refer them for further imaging. A bone scan performed after 48 hours post injury will pick up in some studies 100% of scaphoid fractures. A MRI scan is likewise in some studies 100% sensitive and specific for scaphoid fractures and may detect additional occult soft tissue pathology. This imaging approach will ensure you virtually never miss a scaphoid fracture and will allow the patient to get their definitive treatment and recover more rapidly. CT scans are good at defining fracture displacement but miss approximately 1% of scaphoid fractures and therefore CTs are not ideal for detecting occult fractures. SPLINTAGE - All patients with a possible or definite scaphoid fracture should have their wrist splinted. There is good evidence support splint type. I would recommend a “Colles” type backslab for a suspected scaphoid fracture and fractures in the first week and then a “Colles” type full cast thereafter if conservative management is pursued. There is no advantage to immobilisation of the thumb, fingers or elbow. CONSERVATIVE MANAGEMENT - patients who have an undisplaced fracture, no fracture comminution, a scaphoid waist or distal pole fracture, who were splinted within four week of injury and who would accept a prolonged cast time can be considered for cast treatment. Immobilisation should be continued until the fracture is clinically and radiologically united which normally takes at least 9 weeks and usually longer. If conservative management is to be considered I would suggest a CT scan to confirm that the fracture is genuinely undisplaced and thereafter x-rays at six weeks and then fortnightly thereafter to assess fracture healing. If there is no evidence of healing by eight weeks then a change to surgical management should be contemplated. SURGICAL MANAGEMENT – when the fracture is displaced, comminuted, is a proximal pole fracture or has not been splinted following the injury within four week then union rates reduce and plaster treatment is not ideal. Surgical management has progressed substantially and it is usually possible to percutaneous or arthroscopically fix the fracture with excellent recovery times and union rates. Patients who find prolonged casting unacceptable would benefit from fixation. Based on the above outline the majority of patients should undergo surgical treatment for their scaphoid fractures. OTHER PATHOLOGY - As MRI and wrist arthroscopy has become more advanced it has become evident that many patients sustain soft tissue wrist injuries which often are best treated early, and therefore if your index of suspicion is high due to the level of symptoms or signs despite the absence of a fracture then investigation is warranted. Picture 1 – scaphoid fracture on CT Figure 1 – management guidelines flow chart
